article_title: MiniMax Releases M3, an Open-Weight Model With a 1-Million-Token Context That It Says Tops GPT-5.5 on SWE-Bench Pro

content_preview.title: MiniMax Releases M3, an Open-Weight Model With a 1-Million-Token Context That It Says Tops GPT-5.5 on SWE-Bench Pro
content_preview.summary: Shanghai-based MiniMax launched M3 on June 1, pairing a 1-million-token context with a new sparse-attention design and company benchmarks that top GPT-5.5, with weights promised within 10 days.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Chinese AI start-up MiniMax debuted a new model, M3, on June 1, 2026, positioning it as an open-weight system aimed at long and complex coding tasks, according to the [South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/tech/tech-trends/article/3355529/minimax-debuts-ai-model-built-long-and-complex-coding-tasks). The model is billed as the first open-weight model to combine top-tier coding performance, a one-million-token context window, and native multimodality, [The Decoder reported](h...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (592 words, in range). Clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis arc. Technical depth is appropriate and the article consistently frames every benchmark as company- or vendor-reported, with explicit caveats about the absence of independent verification at launch.
editor_notes.source_verification: All three source snapshots read from disk (gunzip -c). source-0 (SCMP, status 200): confirms M3 debuted 1 Jun 2026, 1M tokens 'five times more than its predecessor, the M2.7', architecture cut computational requirements to 'as little as one-twentieth of previous levels' slashing inference costs, beat GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on SWE-Bench Pro, did NOT disclose model size or training infrastructure. source-1 (MarkTechPost, status 200): confirms SWE-Bench Pro 59.0% (surpasses GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, approaches Opus 4.7), Terminal-Bench 2.1 66.0%, OSWorld-Verified 70.06%; per-token compute '1/20th that of the previous-generation M2 models', '>9x in the prefill stage and >15x in the decoding stage at 1M-token context'; weights+technical report open-sourced within 10 days; 'image and video input and desktop computer operation natively', 'mixed-modality training from step 0'; benchmarks tested on internal infrastructure with Claude Code scaffolding. source-2 (The Decoder, status 200): confirms 'first open-weight model to combine top-tier coding performance, a one-million-token context window, and native multimodality'; BrowseComp 83.5 vs Opus 4.7's 79.3; MSA processes selected segments not every token pair; pricing requests up to 512,000 input tokens at standard rate with longer contexts costing more, token plan $20/mo for ~1.7B tokens up to $120 for 9.8B tokens; weights+report published on Hugging Face and GitHub.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific verified against the snapshots. Predecessor attribution is correctly distinguished: the 5x context expansion is attributed to the M2.7 (SCMP) while the 1/20 per-token compute and >9x prefill / >15x decode speedups are attributed to the M2 (MarkTechPost) — both attributions match their respective sources exactly. All benchmark numbers (59.0, 66.0, 70.06, 83.5/79.3) are exact. Pricing figures exact. June 1 2026 date and 10-day weights timeline correct. No direct quotes appear in the article, so no quote-verbatim risk. Headline ('tops GPT-5.5 on SWE-Bench Pro'), summary, and lead are each sourced and each correctly framed as a company claim ('that It Says', 'company benchmarks').
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism. The 'What We Don't Know' section and the SWE-Bench Pro caution (linking the Herald's prior reporting on systematic grading errors in that benchmark) demonstrate appropriate editorial skepticism toward vendor-run benchmarks rather than amplifying them.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior MiniMax M3 article exists. The internal link to the 2026-05-29 DeepSWE/SWE-Bench Pro piece is used as context, not duplicated. Distinct from prior open-weight coverage (Moonshot/Kimi, DeepSeek, Qwen).
concerns: Two minor cross-citation imprecisions, neither rising to a correction: (1) the sentence on Hugging Face/GitHub publication is attributed to MarkTechPost, but MarkTechPost states only 'open-sourced within 10 days' while The Decoder explicitly names Hugging Face and GitHub; (2) the multimodal 'image and video input / operate a desktop computer natively' specifics are attributed to The Decoder, but those exact specifics appear in MarkTechPost (The Decoder confirms the mixed-modality framing). In both cases the underlying fact is true and traces to one of the two cited sources, both of which are in article.sources, so the provenance chain is intact.
recommendations: Where a specific (HF/GitHub target; video-input / desktop-operation) is stated by a different cited source than the one named in-line, attribute it to the source that actually contains the specific, to keep each in-line citation exact.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, well-sourced submission. Every specific (benchmark numbers, 1M context, speedup multipliers and their correct predecessor attribution, pricing, dates) verified against the on-disk snapshots, and the article consistently and correctly frames the benchmarks as vendor-reported and not independently verified. The only blemishes are two cross-citation imprecisions where a true fact is attributed to one cited source though it appears verbatim in another cited source — both URLs are in article.sources, the facts are accurate, and the provenance chain is unbroken, so this does not warrant a correction. APPROVE.
